threats.title: Retroactive Reward Manipulation via Incorrect Epoch Time in Market Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: The LendingLedger's update_market function incorrectly uses the current block's estimated timestamp for all historical epoch iterations in its catch-up loop, allowing attackers to retroactively apply current gauge weights to past un-updated epochs.
threats.investigation_rationale: This flaw allows an attacker to manipulate current gauge weights and instantly steal a massive share of historical rewards that should have been distributed according to past weights, severely depleting the protocol's treasury.

proof_of_signal.pattern: epochTime is calculated using block.number instead of the loop variable i

preconditions: A market has not had update_market called for at least one epoch
Attacker has sufficient veCANTO to significantly increase the market's gauge weight

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Historical catch-up loop uses current state (block.number) for time-dependent calculations
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None, any user can call update_market
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a market has pending un-updated epochs

operational_questions: Can an attacker profitably acquire veCANTO, vote, and trigger update_market in a single transaction or short window?
How many epochs typically pass between update_market calls in production?
validation_requirements: Simulate a delayed update_market call after a significant gauge weight change
Verify that rewards are distributed using the new weight rather than the historical weight

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a lending market with significant pending rewards (update_market not called recently)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Acquire veCANTO and vote heavily for the target market's gauge to maximize its relative weight
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call update_market for the target market
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires capital to lock for veCANTO or existing voting power

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Severe depletion of the protocol's CANTO reward treasury
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen rewards cannot be recovered without a hard fork
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Reward distribution for all lending markets

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Sufficient CANTO to lock for meaningful voting power
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - can be executed entirely on-chain
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic understanding of smart contract interaction
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Requires finding a market with delayed updates

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Sudden large shifts in gauge weights followed immediately by update_market calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Discrepancy between expected historical emissions and actual claimed amounts
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users voting and immediately triggering market updates

threats.title: Permanent Denial of Service on Gauge Removal via Integer Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: The remove_gauge function zeroes out the gauge type before calling _remove_gauge_weight, causing an integer underflow when attempting to subtract the gauge's weight from the type's total sum, permanently breaking the removal process.
threats.investigation_rationale: Governance relies on remove_gauge to deprecate malicious or inactive markets. This bug permanently prevents governance from removing any gauge that has a positive weight, breaking core administrative workflows.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gauge_types_[_gauge] = 0 is set before _remove_gauge_weight reads it

preconditions: Governance attempts to remove a gauge
The gauge has a positive weight (old_weight_bias > 0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State variable cleared before being passed to an internal cleanup function that relies on it
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always active for any gauge with weight

operational_questions: Is there any alternative way to remove a gauge?
Can governance force a gauge's weight to 0 before removal?
validation_requirements: Deploy a gauge, add weight, and attempt to call remove_gauge
Verify the transaction reverts due to uint256 underflow

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker observes a malicious or deprecated gauge that governance wants to remove
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker ensures the gauge has at least 1 wei of weight (by voting for it)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The gauge remains active and continues to receive emissions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Minimal voting power required to maintain >0 weight

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Governance loses ability to manage gauge lifecycle
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Emissions continue to flow to deprecated or malicious markets
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade or migration

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time before governance attempts removal

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Deprecated gauges continuing to accrue rewards

threats.title: Permanent Loss of User Voting Power on Removed Gauges
threats.hypothesis_description: Users cannot reclaim voting power allocated to removed gauges because attempting to vote 0 triggers an underflow in the gauge type calculation, reverting the transaction and permanently locking their voting capacity.
threats.investigation_rationale: This flaw permanently penalizes users who participated in governance for a gauge that is later removed, reducing their effective voting power forever and breaking the economic incentive model.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gauge_type = gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] - 1 underflows to -1 for removed gauges, failing require(gauge_type >= 0)

preconditions: User has allocated voting power to a gauge
Governance successfully removes the gauge (e.g., if it had 0 weight at the time)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Contradictory require statements: one allows voting 0 on removed gauges, the next reverts because the gauge is removed
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires governance to remove a gauge

operational_questions: How much voting power is currently locked in removed gauges?
Can users bypass this by increasing their lock amount?
validation_requirements: Vote for a gauge, remove the gauge, and attempt to vote 0
Verify the transaction reverts and vote_user_power remains consumed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Governance removes a gauge (assuming H2 is bypassed or gauge had 0 weight)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users attempt to reallocate their voting power by voting 0 on the removed gauge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transactions revert, permanently trapping the allocated basis points
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users' total voting capacity is permanently reduced

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users complaining about inability to vote full 10000 bps

threats.title: Market Bricking via accCantoPerShare uint128 Overflow
threats.hypothesis_description: The reward accumulator casts its per-block increment to uint128. A minimal market supply (e.g., 1 wei) causes the increment to exceed the uint128 maximum, permanently reverting all market operations.
threats.investigation_rationale: An attacker can intentionally brick any newly whitelisted market with a trivial amount of capital, preventing all users from depositing, withdrawing, or claiming rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint128 cast of large division result (blockDelta * cantoPerBlock * weight) / marketSupply

preconditions: Market supply is extremely low (e.g., 1 wei)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Explicit downcast to uint128 without overflow protection on a division by a potentially tiny denominator
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Immediately after a market is whitelisted or if supply drops near zero

operational_questions: What is the exact block delta required to overflow uint128 given standard emission rates?
Can an attacker force market supply to 1 wei?
validation_requirements: Simulate a market with 1 wei supply and standard emissions
Advance blocks and trigger update_market to verify overflow revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Immediately deposit 1 wei of underlying tokens to become the sole supplier
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for sufficient blocks to pass (typically a few hundred)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call update_market, causing a permanent uint128 overflow revert
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires being the first depositor or forcing supply low

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Target market is permanently bricked; no deposits, withdrawals, or claims possible
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires governance to whitelist a new replacement market
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All ledger operations for the specific market

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Markets with extremely low supply and failed update_market transactions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to interact with a specific market
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Attacker depositing 1 wei into new markets

threats.title: User Funds Frozen When Market is Un-whitelisted
threats.hypothesis_description: Un-whitelisting a market causes update_market to revert. Because withdrawing underlying tokens from a gauge triggers update_market, governance un-whitelisting a market permanently traps all user deposits.
threats.investigation_rationale: Governance actions intended to safely pause or deprecate a market will inadvertently cause a catastrophic denial of service, permanently freezing all user principal currently deposited in the gauge.
preconditions: Users have deposited underlying tokens in a LiquidityGauge

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Withdrawal path depends on an administrative whitelist check
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires governance to un-whitelist a market

operational_questions: Is there any alternative withdrawal path that bypasses the ledger sync?
Does governance documentation warn against un-whitelisting active markets?
validation_requirements: Attempt to withdraw tokens and verify revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Governance identifies a problematic market and decides to stop emissions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Governance calls whiteListLendingMarket to set status to false
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users attempt to call withdrawUnderlying to rescue their funds
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All withdrawal transactions revert, trapping funds in the gauge

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete freeze of user principal in deprecated markets
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - governance must re-whitelist the market to allow withdrawals

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed withdrawUnderlying transactions on un-whitelisted markets
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: User complaints about trapped funds after market deprecation

threats.title: Permanent Loss of Underlying Tokens via Unprotected ERC20 Burn
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidityGauge inherits ERC20Burnable without overriding burn(). Calling burn() destroys gauge tokens and reduces the user's ledger balance, but leaves the underlying tokens permanently trapped in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users or integrating smart contracts that call standard ERC20 burn functions will permanently lose their underlying deposits, as the gauge lacks a mechanism to return them outside of withdrawUnderlying.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Inherits ERC20Burnable without overriding to return underlying tokens

preconditions: User or contract calls burn() or burnFrom() instead of withdrawUnderlying()

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Inherited public burn functions bypass the custom withdrawal logic

operational_questions: Are there any protocol integrations that might call standard burn functions?
Can trapped underlying tokens be recovered by any admin function?
validation_requirements: Call burn() on the gauge tokens
Verify that gauge tokens are destroyed but underlying tokens are not returned

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User or third-party vault contract integrates with LiquidityGauge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Integration uses standard ERC20 burn() to exit positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Gauge tokens are burned and ledger syncs the reduction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Underlying tokens remain permanently locked in the LiquidityGauge contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Calls to burn() or burnFrom() on LiquidityGauge contracts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Gauge underlying balance exceeding gauge total supply

threats.title: Missing Delegation Functionality Renders Core Feature Inaccessible
threats.hypothesis_description: The protocol defines delegation structures and handles delegated locks, but lacks any external functions to actually delegate voting power, rendering the feature dead code and preventing intended governance mechanics.
threats.investigation_rationale: A core governance feature implied by the architecture is completely missing, preventing users from executing delegation strategies and leaving dead code paths in critical functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: LockAction enum has DELEGATE but no external function implements it

preconditions: Users attempt to delegate voting power to another address

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Data structures and internal logic exist for delegation, but no entry points expose it

operational_questions: Was delegation intentionally removed or accidentally omitted?
Do any frontends or documentation instruct users to delegate?
validation_requirements: Review entire VotingEscrow contract for any state transition that modifies delegatee after creation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users attempt to participate in governance delegation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users discover no function exists to change the delegatee
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Governance centralization risks increase as delegation is impossible

threats.title: Infinite Voting Power Generation via unlockOverride Sybil Attack
threats.hypothesis_description: If unlockOverride is enabled, users can withdraw locked CANTO without their gauge votes being cancelled. An attacker can repeatedly lock, vote, withdraw, and transfer to a new address to generate infinite voting power.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks the fundamental economic invariant of the voting-escrow model. If governance enables early withdrawals during an emergency, an attacker can instantly hijack all protocol emissions with minimal capital.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw allows early exit if unlockOverride is true, without notifying GaugeController to cancel votes

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Withdrawal logic does not cross-contract call GaugeController to clear active votes

operational_questions: Under what conditions would governance enable unlockOverride?
Is there any off-chain monitoring that would detect rapid lock/vote/withdraw cycles?
validation_requirements: Lock CANTO, vote for a gauge, and withdraw
Verify gauge weight remains high despite the withdrawal

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Governance enables unlockOverride (e.g., during a protocol migration or emergency)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker locks CANTO from Address A and votes for their preferred gauge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker withdraws CANTO from Address A (votes remain active)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker transfers CANTO to Address B and repeats the process, multiplying voting power infinitely
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires scripting to execute rapid Sybil loops

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All protocol rewards stolen by attacker-controlled markets
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires disabling override and manually correcting gauge weights

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Rapid sequence of createLock -> vote_for_gauge_weights -> withdraw -> transfer
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Total gauge weight vastly exceeding total locked CANTO
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Sybil addresses performing identical lock/vote/withdraw loops
